text: The robotic surgical procedure involves the removal of the vagina in a male patient with XL-DESD. A cystoscope is inserted into the urethra, and the vaginal opening is identified at the level of the pulpal urethra. The epithelium of the vagina is visible. A ureteral catheter is inserted into the vagina and left in place to aid in locating it during the abdominal approach.

The robotic phase of the procedure begins with a longitudinal incision in the parietal peritoneum, exposing the bladder. The bladder is then suspended on the anterior abdominal wall. The cystoscope light is visible, guiding the identification of structures within the pelvic space.

A traction suture is placed on the apex of the vagina. The surgeon begins liberating the vagina from adhesions, starting on the right side and then moving to the left. The dissection continues until the attachment to the urethra is reached. The vagina is now completely freed from surrounding adhesions, revealing the urethral catheter inside.

The surgeon removes the right part of the vaginal tissue, placing a stitch at the level of the stump. The left part is then excised, followed by the placement of an additional stitch to reinforce the suture line. Hemostatic foam is applied to the operating site to control bleeding at the end of the procedure.
